content.full_text:
CITY OF
LOMPOC
VALLEY OF FLOWERS

March 12 8:55 AM '90

March 8, 1990

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 South J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Advice Relating to Potential Conflict of Interests

Dear General Counsel:

In order to assist two members of the City Council of the City of
Lompoc, our office has concluded that it would be desirable to
request written advice from your office in accordance with
Government Code §83114, regarding conflict of interest questions.
Both Councilmembers, J.D. Smith and Christa Marks, have expressly
authorized this request for an advice letter. For your assistance,
I will set forth the facts and circumstances giving rise to this
request. I am also attaching relevant documents for your
reference.

Introduction and Overview.

A recent development proposal in the City's medium density, R-2
zone, is creating a certain amount of controversy. Briefly, the
developer's proposal (hereinafter referred to for convenience as
"the Project") involves taking a 10,500 square foot lot that
currently has two existing units on it, adding a third unit, and
subdividing the units for ownership purposes. The procedures that
he is using includes application of what we call our "PD" zone as
an overlay over the R-2, medium density designation. Without going
into details unnecessary for your evaluation, simply put the "PD"
zoning concept allows flexibility in meeting design standards,
which is necessary in order for the applicant to do this project.
(Reference Sections 7700 et seq. of the Lompoc City Code, copies
of which are attached.) Although these zoning and land use
development procedures have existed in the City's Code for many
years, my understanding is that the Project represents a new way
of using these development tools in the City of Lompoc.

The Project has been vigorously opposed by a large group of
citizens. In addition to criticizing the specific design, as it
relates to the distance between the proposed units and other

CITY HALL, 100 CIVIC CENTER PLAZA, P.O. BOX 8001, LOMPOC, CA 93438-8001
(805) 736-1261; FAX: (805) 736-5347

March 8, 1990
Page 2


features, the opponents have expressed considerable concern
regarding the "precedence" that will be set if the Project is
allowed to proceed. They have asserted that others with 10,500
square foot lots with only two units, could do the same thing.
Others have expressed concern about the wisdom of using the PDP
on small lots in existing neighborhoods. Accordingly, on January
2, 1990 the Lompoc City Council adopted a moratorium on
developments in the R-2 zone and the use of the "PDP" concept. The
Project was referred back to Staff and the Planning Commission for
their review. Since that time, the moratorium has also been
extended to the R-3, high-density residential zone.

Both the Project and a series of possible amendments to the Zoning
Ordinance are now working their way back to the City Council for
their consideration. (See attached report to Planning Commission,
which details the proposed amendments to the Zoning Ordinance.)
It has now been brought to light that two City Councilmembers own
property zoned R-2 and R-3. The question has arisen as to whether
they can participate when the City Council considers both the
Project and the proposed changes to the Zoning Ordinance.
Accordingly, written advice from your office is being sought to
clarify the question of those councilmembers' participation.

Property Owned by Councilmember Marks.

Councilmember Marks owns two properties that may be affected.
First, she is the owner of a site that is 17,500 square feet that
is zoned R-3. The lot consists of her residence and two additional
rental units. Under the density standards for the R-3 zone in the
existing Code, Planning Department staff has indicated she could
development a maximum of eight dwelling units on the site. Her
residence also has been designated a local historical landmark,
which results in certain restrictions being imposed on her house.
These restrictions do not extend to the rest of the property
(reference attached Resolution 3495(85). The attached map of the
City may be of assistance in putting the location of her residence
in perspective (the pencil drawn circle is a 2500 sq. ft. radius
around her property).

Councilmember Marks also owns a four-unit rental property that is
zoned R-2. I am informed by the Planning Department staff that the
lot area consists of 7,000 square feet. The current standards for
the R-2 zone allow one dwelling unit for each 3,000 square feet of
lot area. Based upon this standard, only two dwelling units would
be allowed to be developed on the site. Accordingly, the property
has a "legal, non-conforming" status. Staff has further advised
that under the Zoning Ordinance provisions relating to non-
conforming uses and structures, they may be continued but not

March 8, 1990
Page 3


expanded. The Planning Department staff has also advised that in
their view, properties that contain non-conforming uses and/or
structures would not be eligible for development using the "PDD"
zoning designation (see attached memorandum from Acting Associate
Planner Arlen Dellister). No changes to the Zoning Ordinance,
relating to legal, non-conforming uses and structures, are being
proposed at this time.

Both of the properties owned by Councilmember Marks are located
more than 2500 feet from the Project. In addition, while a range
of possible Zoning Ordinance Amendments have been prepared for
Planning Commission and city Council consideration, the actual
densities, as far as the number of units that can be developed on
a site, are not being proposed to be increased or decreased.
Depending on what other changes are made to the Code, the
development potential of the R-3 site could be affected. As to the
legal, non-conforming R-2 property, the Planning Department has
advised that it does not appear that any of the changes being
proposed would change its development potential.

Property Owned by Councilmember Smith.

Councilmember Smith owns a lot that is zoned R-2 that consists of
7,000 square feet. According to Councilmember Smith, there are
three existing units on the site. Based upon the density standards
for the R-2 zone, this property could only be developed with two
units, therefore it is also "legal, non-conforming." Thus, the
analysis presented for the legal, non-conforming property owned by
Councilmember Marks, also applies to Councilmember Smith's
property.

Councilmember Smith's property is also located more than 2500 feet
from the proposed development project.

Application of Regulations to Possible Conflicts.

I realize the difficulty in analyzing the foregoing in relation to
the FPPC Regulations. Certainly, one problem will be that your
office is generally unfamiliar with the City's Zoning Ordinance.
Accordingly, the following observations are offered for your
assistance.

We recognize that there are two separate matters that need to be
analyzed. The first is the ability of the Councilmembers to
participate on a decision for the Project. The second is the
question of changes to the Zoning Ordinance that apply generally
to all properties situated in the city that are zoned R-2 and R-3.

March 8, 1990
Page 4


Section 18702.3 -- Materiality and Property Indirectly Involved in
the Decision.

As noted, all three of the properties in question are located more
than 2500 feet from the Project. Therefore, it would appear that
Section 18702.3(b), of the Regulations of the Fair Political
Practices Commission, is controlling in determining whether the
effect of a decision on the Project will be material.

As to the properties owned by Councilmembers Marks and Smith that
are zoned legal, non-conforming, the Planning Department staff has
concluded that their status precludes developments as a "PD"
project. Therefore, it would not appear that a decision could
affect the market value of the property in the amount set forth in
Section 18702.3(a)(3)(A) or (a)(3)(B), or otherwise indirectly
effect the property.

I find a decision on the Project and its potential effect on
Councilmember Marks' R-3 property, that has the potential for as
many as eight units, more difficult to analyze. At first blush,
all the talk about the "precedence" of the decision would seem to
suggest that action on the Project may indeed impact the value of
this piece of property. It should be noted, however, that this
property is already zoned at a higher density, and is large enough
to allow for additional development, regardless of what happens to
the Project. Moreover, although there is a lot of talk of
"precedence," the fact is that the opportunity to use the existing
City Codes to develop the property has always been there. The "PD"
zone is site specific, and considered on a case by case basis.
Councilmember Marks could always have applied to develop the
property under the existing regulations. In any event, I assume
that the answer will be a technical one that has to be answered by
an appraiser or real estate professional. The question, as I
understand it, is whether the value of the property will be
increased or decreased in the amounts set forth in Sections
18702.3(a)(3)(A) or (a)(3)(B), as a result of action on the
Project. In any event, I have advised Councilmember Marks to
discuss her property, and possible financial effect of any decision
on the Project, with real estate professionals and other that might
be knowledgeable in that regard.

Also, under Section 18702.3(b), in addition to the threshold
financial effect, either the effect must not be substantially the
same as the effect of at least 25% of properties within a 2500 foot
radius of the property in question, or there are not at least ten
properties under separate ownership within 2500 feet. I am
informed by the Planning Department staff that they are unable to
provide any analysis regarding this second element. Although there

March 8, 1990
Page 5


are in all likelihood more than ten properties within 2500 feet
under separate ownership (we have determined that there are 410
parcels in that area), the Planning Department staff has indicated
that there are practical problems with determining the development
potential of property located within 2500 feet. In all candor, in
their view it is an unworkable formula.

Section 18702.1 -- Material Financial Effect on Property Directly
Involved.

As to the effect of the proposed changes to the Zoning Ordinance,
it would appear that Section 18702.1 is controlling. In discussing
this with one of your staff attorneys, however, it was indicated
that Section 18702.3 relating to property indirectly involved in
a decision might also come into play. Frankly, I am somewhat
confused by this. I also, however, understand that these
provisions are relatively new, and have not undergone extensive
application to factual situations.

The Code changes that are being discussed, in my view, directly
involve the properties in question. This is because they are the
rules that will govern the manner in which these properties may be
developed and used. Section 18702.1(a)(3)(B) appears to
specifically exempt amendments to zoning ordinances or other land
use regulations that are applicable to all other properties
designated in a particular zoning category, from rezoning and
rezoning actions that might effect property directly involved in
a decision. That would appear to be the case in the instant
matter, since what is contemplated are changes to the rules and
regulations that would apply to all R-2 and R-3 properties in the
City.

I am unclear how Section 18702.3(c) might also apply. That section
provides that where the official's property is indirectly involved,
and the decision does not involve a subject property from which
distances can be determined, the threshold monetary standards
contained in Subdivision (a)(3) apply. If this is the case, it is
inconceivable that a specific decision on the rules and
regulations for development projects in the R-2 and R-3 would have
the required monetary impact on Councilmember Marks' R-3 property.
This appears to lead to the question of the role of Section 18703,
relating to effect on the public generally, and how it enters into
the analysis.

Section 18703 -- Effect on the Public Generally.

If I understand things correctly, even if there is a material
financial effect, disqualification is not required if the

March 8, 1990
Page 6


official's interest is effected in a substantially similar manner
as a significant segment of the public. I am unclear as to how
this rule is to be applied.

For your assistance, the following facts, according to the Planning
Department, are provided: The City has a population of
approximately 33,600. Its area covers 10.94 square miles, and it
contains approximately 7004 acres. It is divided into several
commercial, industrial and residential zones. There are four
residential zones (RA, R-1, R-2 and R-3). There are approximately
199.66 acres of R-2 property and 204.34 acres of R-3.
I am
informed by the Planning Department that based upon a study
conducted in January, 1989, there are 492 parcels of land in the
City zoned R-2 and 608 zoned R-3. This study also determined that
208 R-2 lots and 254 R-3 lots had "development potential." This
was based upon the Planning Department's analysis that additional
units could be added to a parcel, based upon what has been built,
as compared to the density allowed by the Zoning Ordinance.

A decision on the Project arguably will effect all of those parcels
that have "development potential" in a similar manner. Yet, each
piece of property will have its own characteristics (i.e., size,
shape, condition and nature of existing structures, etc.) that
might skew this conclusion. The dilemma appears to be how far to
go in analyzing the question of "similar" effect when dealing with
a land use/zoning question in relation to property that is
something other than raw land. On the surface, however, every
property in the City in the same zoning category would appear to
be impacted in a similar manner.

It was also suggested to me that a no vote on the Project, or
disallowing development similar to the Project, could have the
effect of increasing the value of existing units (i.e. if
opportunities to develop additional housing is restricted, existing
housing is more valuable). My sense is that the answer to this
point also lies in Section 18703. Everyone owning R-2 and R-3
units (and indeed R-1, single family houses) are effected the same
way.

Finally, the foregoing considerations also seem to apply to changes
to the Zoning Ordinance that are applicable to all property in the
City in the same zoning category. In fact, as you will note from
the attached report detailing the staff recommended changes to the
Code, proposed "Development Review" process would apply to
virtually all properties in the City (the only exception being a
single family home on an individual lot). What is meant by Section
18703 has significant implications beyond the instant question,
since cities are frequently involved in changes to General Plans

March 8, 1990
Page 7


and Zoning Ordinances that apply to the City as a whole (as opposed
to rezoning a particular parcel of property). If certain actions
on specific provisions to the Zoning Ordinance might be construed
as a conflict, how can that be handled in any practical way?
Accordingly, I hope that your office's approach to this issue
reflects a recognition of the need for practical, workable formulas
and rules.

I hope the foregoing thoughts will be of assistance. On behalf of
Councilmembers Marks and Smith, thank you for your office's
assistance with regard to providing guidance so that improper
conduct is avoided. If either my office or the Planning
Department, can provide you with any additional clarification
regarding the foregoing, please feel free to contact me at
(805) 736-1261, ext. 231.

Very truly yours,

David H. Hirsch
City Attorney

cc: Councilmember Marks
    Councilmember Smith

For your assistance, the folLoi^'ing facts, according to the Plahning

The city has a population of
approximately 33,600. Its area covers 10.94 square rniles, and it

residential zones (LA, R-1, R-2 and R-3). There are approxihately
1.99.66 acres of R-2 property and 204.34 acres of R-3. I atn

208 R-2 lots and 254 R-3 lots had rtdevelopnent potential.I 
This
lias based upon the Planning Departu[ent's analysis that additional
units could be added to a parcel, based upon what has been built,
as compared to the density allowed by the Zoninq Ordinance.
A decision on the Project arquably rri1l effect all of those parcels

night skev this conclusion. The dilerula appears to be how far to

sornething other than ralr land. On the surface, however, every

It lras also suggested to ne that a no vote on the project, or
disal"lorring develophent sinilar to the Project, could have the

opportunities to develop additional housing is restricted, existing
housing is nore valuable). lIy sense is that the ansrrer to thia
point also lies in Section 1.8703. Everyone olrning R-2 and R-3
units (and indeed R-1, single family houses) are effacted the same
vay.
Fina11y, the foregoing considerations also seem to apply to changes
to the Zoning Ordinance that are appticable to all pioperty in tne
city in the same zoning cateqory. In fact, as you-will note fron
the attached report detailing the staff recommen-ded changes to the
Code, the proposed fDevelopment Reviewr process vou]d apply to
virtually att properties it the city (the only exception lli-"g u
single family home on an individual fbt). wnat-is meant by section
18703 has. significant implications beyond the instant [uestion,
slnce cities are frequently involved j.n changes to ceneral plans


Uarch 8, L99o
and Zoning Ordj.nances that apply to the City as a whole (as opposed
to rezoning a particutar parcel of property). If certain actions
on specific provisions to the Zoning ordinance uright be construed

how can that be handled in any practical way?
Accordingrly, I hope that your officers approach to this issue

embedding.first_500_words: CITY OF LOMPOC VALLEY OF FLOWERS March 12 8:55 AM '90 March 8, 1990 General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 South J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Request for Advice Relating to Potential Conflict of Interests Dear General Counsel: In order to assist two members of the City Council of the City of Lompoc, our office has concluded that it would be desirable to request written advice from your office in accordance with Government Code §83114, regarding conflict of interest questions. Both Councilmembers, J.D. Smith and Christa Marks, have expressly authorized this request for an advice letter. For your assistance, I will set forth the facts and circumstances giving rise to this request. I am also attaching relevant documents for your reference. Introduction and Overview. A recent development proposal in the City's medium density, R-2 zone, is creating a certain amount of controversy. Briefly, the developer's proposal (hereinafter referred to for convenience as "the Project") involves taking a 10,500 square foot lot that currently has two existing units on it, adding a third unit, and subdividing the units for ownership purposes. The procedures that he is using includes application of what we call our "PD" zone as an overlay over the R-2, medium density designation. Without going into details unnecessary for your evaluation, simply put the "PD" zoning concept allows flexibility in meeting design standards, which is necessary in order for the applicant to do this project. (Reference Sections 7700 et seq. of the Lompoc City Code, copies of which are attached.) Although these zoning and land use development procedures have existed in the City's Code for many years, my understanding is that the Project represents a new way of using these development tools in the City of Lompoc. The Project has been vigorously opposed by a large group of citizens. In addition to criticizing the specific design, as it relates to the distance between the proposed units and other CITY HALL, 100 CIVIC CENTER PLAZA, P.O. BOX 8001, LOMPOC, CA 93438-8001 (805) 736-1261; FAX: (805) 736-5347 March 8, 1990 Page 2 features, the opponents have expressed considerable concern regarding the "precedence" that will be set if the Project is allowed to proceed. They have asserted that others with 10,500 square foot lots with only two units, could do the same thing. Others have expressed concern about the wisdom of using the PDP on small lots in existing neighborhoods. Accordingly, on January 2, 1990 the Lompoc City Council adopted a moratorium on developments in the R-2 zone and the use of the "PDP" concept. The Project was referred back to Staff and the Planning Commission for their review. Since that time, the moratorium has also been extended to the R-3, high-density residential zone. Both the Project and a series of possible amendments to the Zoning Ordinance are now working their way back to the City Council for their consideration. (See attached report to Planning Commission, which details the proposed amendments to the Zoning Ordinance.) It has now
